rows.title: Federal Trade Commission Act: US Federal Statute Establishing the FTC and Prohibiting Unfair or Deceptive Acts and Practices
rows.summary:
The Federal Trade Commission Act (FTC Act), 15 U.S.C. §§ 41–58, is the foundational federal statute of the United States that created the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and confers upon it authority to prohibit unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.

Originally enacted on 26 September 1914 by the 63rd United States Congress, the FTC Act established the Commission as an independent federal agency. The statute has been amended on multiple occasions, most notably by the Wheeler-Lea Act of 1938, which extended the FTC's jurisdiction to cover unfair or deceptive acts and practices regardless of whether they harm competition directly, thereby protecting consumers as a distinct class.

Section 5 of the FTC Act, codified at 15 U.S.C. § 45, constitutes the statute's primary operative provision. It declares unlawful unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. The Commission is empowered under Section 5 to issue cease-and-desist orders following administrative proceedings. Section 13(b), codified at 15 U.S.C. § 53(b), authorises the Commission to seek injunctive relief in federal district court; the scope of this provision, particularly the availability of equitable monetary relief, has been the subject of sustained litigation, most recently addressed by the Supreme Court of the United States in AMG Capital Management, LLC v. FTC, 593 U.S. 67 (2021), in which the Court held unanimously that Section 13(b) does not authorise the FTC to seek equitable monetary relief such as restitution or disgorgement.

Section 6 of the FTC Act grants the Commission investigative and reporting powers, including the authority to require corporations to file annual and special reports and to investigate the organisation, business, conduct, and practices of entities subject to its jurisdiction. Section 18, codified at 15 U.S.C. § 57a, authorises the Commission to promulgate trade regulation rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive.

The FTC Act does not create a private right of action; enforcement authority is vested exclusively in the Commission and, in specified circumstances, in the Department of Justice. The statute exempts certain sectors from FTC jurisdiction, including common carriers subject to the Communications Act, banks, savings institutions, and federal credit unions, which are addressed by sector-specific regulators.

rows.title: Medical Debt Reporting Act: US Federal Legislation to Restrict Credit Reporting of Medical Debt
rows.summary:
The Medical Debt Reporting Act is a federal legislative proposal introduced in the United States Congress that would prohibit consumer reporting agencies from including medical debt information in consumer credit reports.

The bill has been introduced in multiple sessions of Congress, with versions sponsored in both the Senate and the House of Representatives. The legislation targets the practice of reporting unpaid medical bills on consumer credit files, which proponents argue distorts creditworthiness assessments given the involuntary and often unexpected nature of medical expenses.

The Act, as introduced, would amend the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), 15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq., by adding provisions that bar consumer reporting agencies from furnishing reports containing medical debt information. Furnishers of information, including hospitals, collection agencies, and other healthcare creditors, would be prohibited from providing such data to consumer reporting agencies for inclusion in credit files. The legislation applies to all consumer reporting agencies as defined under the existing FCRA framework.

The bill's provisions are directed at a category of debt that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) has separately examined in rulemaking. The CFPB issued a proposed rule in June 2024 that would similarly remove medical debt from credit reports under amendments to Regulation V, which implements the FCRA. The CFPB stated in its rulemaking materials that medical debt on credit reports has limited predictive value for future repayment of non-medical obligations and that its inclusion may result in inaccurate credit assessments.

Under the legislative text as introduced, violations of the prohibition would be enforceable under the existing FCRA civil liability framework, including statutory damages of not less than USD 100 (approximately EUR 92) and not more than USD 1,000 (approximately EUR 920) per violation under 15 U.S.C. § 1681n, as well as actual damages and attorney's fees. Federal agencies and the CFPB would retain their respective supervisory and enforcement authorities over covered entities.

rows.title: United States Patent Act: Federal Statute Governing Patent Rights and Procedures
rows.summary:
The United States Patent Act, codified at Title 35 of the United States Code (35 U.S.C.), is the primary federal statute governing the grant, scope, and enforcement of patent rights across all domestic and international patent matters within United States jurisdiction.

The Patent Act was first enacted by Congress in 1790, with subsequent consolidations in 1836 and 1952 providing the foundational structure that remains in force today. The current codification at 35 U.S.C. was established by the Patent Act of 1952 (Pub. L. 82-593), which restructured patent law into its modern form. The statute has since been amended on numerous occasions, most notably by the Leahy-Smith America Invents Act of 2011 (Pub. L. 112-29), which transitioned the United States from a first-to-invent to a first-inventor-to-file system.

The statute is organised into multiple parts covering distinct areas of patent law. Part I (35 U.S.C. §§ 1-42) establishes the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) and sets out its administrative structure and powers. Part II (35 U.S.C. §§ 100-212) defines patentability requirements, including the conditions set out at 35 U.S.C. § 101 (subject matter eligibility), § 102 (novelty), § 103 (non-obviousness), and § 112 (written description and enablement). Part III (35 U.S.C. §§ 251-329) addresses patent examination procedures, reissue, reexamination, and the inter partes review proceedings introduced by the America Invents Act. Part IV (35 U.S.C. §§ 351-376) implements the Patent Cooperation Treaty obligations of the United States.

The Act provides that patents confer upon the holder the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling the patented invention throughout the United States for a term of twenty years from the filing date of the application, as specified at 35 U.S.C. § 154. Infringement remedies are addressed at 35 U.S.C. §§ 281-299, with damages provisions at § 284 permitting awards of not less than a reasonable royalty. The USPTO, operating within the Department of Commerce, administers the statute and publishes examination guidelines interpreting its provisions.

rows.title: Copyright Act of 1976: United States Federal Copyright Law Codified at Title 17 of the US Code
rows.summary:
The Copyright Act of 1976 is the principal federal statute governing copyright protection in the United States, enacted by the United States Congress and codified at Title 17 of the United States Code, with effect from 1 January 1978.

On 19 October 1976, Congress enacted the Copyright Act of 1976 (Pub. L. 94-553, 90 Stat. 2541), superseding the Copyright Act of 1909 and establishing a comprehensive framework for the creation, ownership, duration, and enforcement of copyright in the United States. The Act came into force on 1 January 1978.

The Act provides that copyright subsists in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, covering categories including literary works, musical works, dramatic works, pictorial and graphic works, motion pictures, and sound recordings (17 U.S.C. § 102). The Act establishes the general duration of copyright protection as the life of the author plus 70 years for works created on or after 1 January 1978 (17 U.S.C. § 302(a)), a term later extended by the Sonny Bono Copyright Term Extension Act of 1998. For works made for hire, anonymous works, and pseudonymous works, the Act provides a term of 95 years from publication or 120 years from creation, whichever expires first (17 U.S.C. § 302(c)).

The Act enumerates the exclusive rights of copyright holders, including the rights to reproduce the work, prepare derivative works, distribute copies, perform the work publicly, and display the work publicly (17 U.S.C. § 106). The Act also codifies the doctrine of fair use at 17 U.S.C. § 107, setting out four factors for courts to weigh when determining whether an unauthorised use of a copyrighted work qualifies as fair use: the purpose and character of the use, the nature of the copyrighted work, the amount and substantiality of the portion used, and the effect of the use upon the potential market for the copyrighted work. The Act further establishes the Copyright Office within the Library of Congress (17 U.S.C. § 701) and provides remedies for infringement including injunctive relief, actual damages, statutory damages ranging from USD 750 to USD 30,000 per work infringed (approximately EUR 690 to EUR 27,600 at prevailing rates), and, in cases of wilful infringement, statutory damages up to USD 150,000 (approximately EUR 138,000) per work (17 U.S.C. § 504).

rows.title: California Civil Code Section 1798.99.82: Protections for Neural Data Under the California AI Transparency and Privacy Framework
rows.summary:
California Civil Code section 1798.99.82 establishes statutory protections for neural data as a category of sensitive personal information under California law, forming part of the state legislature's framework governing the collection, use, and disclosure of data derived from individuals' brain and nervous system activity.

On 15 October 2023, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 1223 (SB 1223) into law, which amended the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) as codified in the California Civil Code to add neural data — defined as information generated by measuring the activity of a consumer's central or peripheral nervous system — to the enumerated categories of sensitive personal information afforded heightened protection. Section 1798.99.82 was introduced as part of a broader legislative package addressing emerging neurotechnology and brain-computer interface applications.

The provision places neural data within the scope of the CCPA's sensitive personal information regime established under Civil Code section 1798.121, meaning that consumers retain the right to direct businesses to limit the use and disclosure of such data to purposes reasonably necessary for providing requested goods or services. The Act provides that businesses collecting neural data must honour opt-out and limitation requests in the same manner as other sensitive categories enumerated in the statute. The legislature determined that neural data warrants this classification because of the intimate and uniquely identifying nature of neurological signals, which can reveal cognitive states, intentions, and health-related information beyond what consumers may knowingly disclose.

Enforcement authority rests with the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and the California Attorney General, consistent with the broader CCPA enforcement structure. Violations are subject to civil penalties of up to USD 2,500 (approximately EUR 2,300) per unintentional violation and up to USD 7,500 (approximately EUR 6,900) per intentional violation or violation involving the personal information of a minor, as provided under Civil Code section 1798.155. The California legislature's amendment reflects growing legislative attention to neurotechnology across multiple United States jurisdictions, including Colorado and Minnesota, which have enacted separate neural or biological data statutes.

rows.title: California Business and Professions Code Section 22757.2: Children's Data Privacy Obligations for Connected Toy Operators
rows.summary:
Section 22757.2 of the California Business and Professions Code imposes specific data privacy obligations on operators of internet-connected toys and devices directed at children, forming part of California's broader Children's Online Privacy Protection framework under the California Consumer Privacy Act and related statutes, with application across the State of California, USA.

Section 22757.2 sits within Division 8, Chapter 22 of the California Business and Professions Code, which addresses privacy protections for minors in digital and connected environments. The provision imposes requirements on operators of internet-connected toys, games, and devices that are directed to children or that knowingly collect personal information from children. The statute defines the scope of covered operators and specifies the categories of personal information subject to its restrictions, including geolocation data, voice recordings, and other data collected through the functionality of connected devices.

The Code provides that operators subject to Section 22757.2 are prohibited from knowingly engaging in certain data practices with respect to children's personal information collected through covered devices. Specifically, the statute restricts the use, disclosure, and retention of such information beyond what is reasonably necessary to provide the requested service or activity. Operators are further prohibited from compiling, disclosing, or retaining a minor's personal information for purposes of targeted advertising or for any purpose not consistent with the context in which the information was collected. The provision also imposes an obligation to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the personal information collected.

Section 22757.2 operates in conjunction with other provisions of the California Business and Professions Code addressing minors' privacy, including Section 22580 and the broader framework established by the California Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (AB 2273, 2022). Enforcement authority rests with the California Attorney General. Violations of the chapter are subject to civil penalties and other remedies available under California law, with individual violations potentially attracting penalties per incident as specified in the broader enforcement provisions of the Code.

rows.title: United States Code Title 17 Section 107: Federal Fair Use Doctrine for Copyright
rows.summary:
Title 17 of the United States Code, Section 107, codifies the doctrine of fair use as a statutory limitation on the exclusive rights of copyright holders under federal law in the United States.

On 19 October 1976, the United States Congress enacted the Copyright Act of 1976 (Public Law 94-553), which came into force on 1 January 1978. Section 107 of that Act, now codified at 17 U.S.C. § 107, established fair use as an express statutory defence to copyright infringement for the first time in United States federal law, drawing on and codifying prior common law development of the doctrine.

The provision states that the fair use of a copyrighted work, including reproduction in copies or phonorecords or by any other means specified in that section, for purposes such as criticism, comment, news reporting, teaching, scholarship, or research, is not an infringement of copyright. Section 107 enumerates four factors that a court must weigh in determining whether a particular use qualifies as fair use: first, the purpose and character of the use, including whether it is of a commercial nature or is for non-profit educational purposes; second, the nature of the copyrighted work; third, the amount and substantiality of the portion used in relation to the copyrighted work as a whole; and fourth, the effect of the use upon the potential market for or value of the copyrighted work. The statute provides that the fact that a work is unpublished shall not itself bar a finding of fair use if the four-factor analysis supports such a finding.

The four-factor test is applied by courts on a case-by-case basis, with no single factor determinative. The United States Supreme Court has addressed the doctrine in a series of rulings, including Campbell v. Acuff-Rose Music, Inc., 510 U.S. 569 (1994), in which the Court confirmed that all four statutory factors must be explored and weighed together, and that commercial purpose does not automatically preclude a finding of fair use. The doctrine has acquired particular relevance in disputes involving text and data mining, artificial intelligence training datasets, and digital reproduction, with multiple federal court proceedings as of 2024 and 2025 examining whether the ingestion of copyrighted works to train large language models falls within the scope of 17 U.S.C. § 107.

rows.title: California Business and Professions Code Section 22757.3: Age-Appropriate Design Requirements for Online Platforms
rows.summary:
California Business and Professions Code section 22757.3 forms part of the California Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (AB 2273), enacted by the California State Legislature and signed into law in September 2022, establishing child-safety design obligations for online services likely to be accessed by minors in the State of California, USA.

On 15 September 2022, Governor Gavin Newsom signed Assembly Bill 2273 into law, adding sections 22757 through 22757.5 to the California Business and Professions Code. The provisions took effect on 1 July 2024, following a statutory delayed implementation period intended to allow covered businesses time to come into compliance.

Section 22757.3 specifically sets out the substantive design obligations imposed on covered businesses, defined under the Act as businesses that provide an online service, product, or feature likely to be accessed by children. The section requires covered businesses to configure default privacy settings to the highest level of privacy available, to refrain from using personal information collected from a child in a manner that the business knows, or has reason to know, is detrimental to the health or well-being of the child, and to provide privacy information, terms of service, policies, and community standards in a manner that is accessible and easy to understand. The Act defines 'child' as a consumer under 18 years of age. The California Attorney General is designated as the enforcement authority, with civil penalties of up to USD 2,500 (approximately EUR 2,300) per affected child for negligent violations and up to USD 7,500 (approximately EUR 6,900) per affected child for intentional violations.

The Act additionally requires covered businesses, under section 22757.3, to complete a Data Protection Impact Assessment before offering any new online service, product, or feature likely to be accessed by children, and to make those assessments available to the California Attorney General upon request. The NetChoice v. Bonta litigation, in which a federal district court issued a preliminary injunction against enforcement in September 2023, affected the statute's implementation timeline. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals subsequently vacated the injunction in part and remanded the matter, leaving the scope of enforceable provisions subject to ongoing judicial proceedings as of the instrument's nominal effective date.

rows.title: US Executive Order on Artificial Intelligence (2025)
rows.summary:
The United States issued an Executive Order on artificial intelligence in 2025, signed by President Donald Trump, directing federal agencies to promote AI development and remove regulatory barriers to AI deployment across the American economy.

On 20 January 2025, President Trump signed Executive Order 14179, titled 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence', published in the Federal Register. The Order revoked the prior Biden administration Executive Order 14110 of October 2023, which had established a framework centred on AI safety evaluations, mandatory reporting requirements for developers of frontier models, and civil-rights-oriented guidance for federal AI procurement.

Executive Order 14179 directs the Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) and the AI and Crypto Czar within the White House to develop a new national AI action plan within 180 days of the Order's signing. The Order instructs federal agencies to identify and remove existing rules, guidance documents, and agency policies deemed to constitute barriers to American AI innovation and competitiveness. Agencies are directed to report such barriers to the OSTP as part of the action-plan development process. The Order frames national AI policy around sustaining and enhancing US leadership in AI, with particular emphasis on economic competitiveness and national security applications.

The Order additionally rescinded guidance documents and memoranda issued pursuant to Executive Order 14110, including Office of Management and Budget (OMB) memoranda M-24-10 and M-24-18, which had set requirements for federal agency AI governance, risk management, and transparency practices. The revocation of those OMB memoranda removed the requirement for federal agencies to designate Chief AI Officers with specific authorities, to publish inventories of AI use cases, and to apply minimum risk-management standards to rights-affecting and safety-affecting AI applications in federal programmes.

rows.title: NIST and CAISI to Convene AI Incident Management Workshop in May 2026
rows.summary:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology, through its centre for AI Standards and Innovation, has announced a workshop on AI incident management to be held on 14 May 2026 at NIST's Gaithersburg campus, intended to inform updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations under America's AI Action Plan.

On 31 March 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology, an agency within the U.S. Department of Commerce, published notice of a one-day workshop scheduled for 14 May 2026, running from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm EDT at the Gaithersburg, Maryland facility. NIST states that the event will bring together leaders from the AI and cybersecurity stakeholder communities, including AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, and government stakeholders.

NIST identifies a class of incidents in which AI systems function as both targets of attack and as sources of risk, particularly within critical infrastructure, cybersecurity, and national security contexts. The workshop agenda, as described by NIST, includes the presentation of a high-level NIST roadmap to advance AI incident response standards and practices, stakeholder engagement on existing playbooks and frameworks, exploration of definitions and taxonomies for AI-related incidents, and identification of gaps in current cybersecurity and AI risk management guidance. NIST further notes that the workshop will address emerging AI incident types beyond cybersecurity, including misuse scenarios.

NIST states that outcomes from the workshop will inform future work by its Information Technology Laboratory and the centre for AI Standards and Innovation, including updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations. The authority describes this engagement as part of its effort to implement America's AI Action Plan and to support national and global alignment on AI incident response. No enforcement action, fine, or legal determination is associated with this announcement.
rows.body_name: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation

rows.title: NIST Convenes Workshop on AI Incident Management Standards and Practices
rows.summary:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology has announced a workshop on AI incident management, to be held on 14 May 2026 at its Gaithersburg campus, aimed at developing coordinated standards and guidance for responding to AI-related incidents across government, industry and academia.

On 31 March 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology, an agency within the U.S. Department of Commerce, published details of the forthcoming workshop, which is scheduled to run from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm EDT. NIST notes that no fines or enforcement actions are associated with this announcement. NIST states that the event will convene stakeholders from the AI and cybersecurity communities to initiate dialogue on scalable approaches to AI incident response.

NIST notes that AI systems are increasingly integral to critical infrastructure, cybersecurity and national security, and that a new class of incidents is emerging in which AI systems function as both targets and sources of risk. The workshop agenda, as set out by NIST, includes the presentation of a high-level NIST roadmap to advance AI incident response standards and practices, engagement with stakeholders on existing playbooks and frameworks, exploration of definitions, lifecycles and taxonomy of AI-related incidents, and identification of gaps in current cybersecurity and AI risk management guidance. NIST further states that the workshop will address emerging AI incident types beyond cybersecurity, including misuse scenarios.

NIST indicates that outcomes from the workshop will inform future work by the Information Technology Laboratory and the centre for AI Standards and Innovation, including updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations, as part of efforts to implement America's AI Action Plan. The intended audience, as identified by NIST, includes AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, cybersecurity professionals and government stakeholders. NIST states that the event is intended to support both national and global alignment on AI incident management.
rows.body_name: National Institute of Standards and Technology

rows.title: NTIA Recommends FCC Seek Public Comment on GPS Signal Protection Rules for Direct-to-Device Satellite Equipment
rows.summary:
The National Telecommunications and Information Administration has issued a formal recommendation to the Federal Communications Commission calling for updated guidance to protect the GPS L1 signal at 1575.42 MHz from interference caused by Mobile Satellite Service Direct-to-Device user equipment operating in the 1610–1660.5 MHz band.

On 6 March 2026, the National Telecommunications and Information Administration, acting through its Office of Spectrum Management, transmitted a recommendation letter to the FCC addressing interference risks posed by Direct-to-Device, or D2D, user equipment operated by Mobile Satellite Service providers. The letter requests that the FCC initiate a public comment process on new or updated guidance designed to protect the GPS L1 signal, which operates at 1575.42 MHz, from emissions generated by D2D devices functioning in the adjacent 1610–1660.5 MHz band.

NTIA recommends that the FCC seek public input on emission limits applicable to D2D user equipment, with a view to establishing protections sufficient to prevent harmful interference to GPS L1 operations. The recommendation identifies a specific operational restriction: NTIA further recommends that D2D devices unable to satisfy certain emission limits be rendered incapable of operating aboard aircraft. NTIA indicates that the letter does not specify the precise numerical emission thresholds that would trigger this restriction, indicating that the parameters are intended to be developed through the FCC's forthcoming public comment process.

NTIA states that the recommendation reflects its role as the principal federal adviser on spectrum policy and its responsibility for coordinating federal government spectrum use with the FCC's oversight of non-federal users. NTIA identifies the GPS L1 frequency as widely used across aviation, navigation, timing, and critical infrastructure applications, and NTIA's letter identifies the proximity of MSS D2D operational frequencies as a potential source of out-of-band emissions affecting that band. NTIA notes that no fine or enforcement action is associated with this recommendation, which is advisory in nature and directed at initiating FCC rulemaking or guidance activity.

rows.title: CAISI Issues Request for Information on Security of AI Agent Systems
rows.summary:
The centre for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) at the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) has published a Request for Information seeking input on the secure development and deployment of AI agent systems, with responses to inform voluntary guidelines and best practices.

On 12 January 2026, the centre for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI), operating within the U.S. Department of Commerce's National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), published a Request for Information (RFI) in the Federal Register (docket no. NIST-2025-0035) concerning security considerations for artificial intelligence agent systems. No fines or penalties are associated with this publication. The comment period closes on 9 March 2026.

CAISI identifies AI agent systems as capable of planning and taking autonomous actions that affect real-world systems or environments. CAISI distinguishes between security risks common to software generally, such as authentication vulnerabilities and memory management flaws, and risks specific to AI agent systems. CAISI identifies the latter category as including threats arising from AI model outputs interacting with adversarial data, such as indirect prompt injection, risks from the use of models subject to data poisoning, and the risk that models may take actions harmful to security even in the absence of adversarial inputs, including through specification gaming or misaligned objectives.

CAISI frames these security challenges as both a barrier to current adoption and a potential risk to public safety and national security as AI agent systems become more widely deployed. The RFI poses questions covering the nature and evolution of unique security threats affecting AI agent systems, methods for improving security during development and deployment, gaps in existing cybersecurity approaches when applied to agent systems, methods for measuring agent security, and interventions in deployment environments to constrain and monitor agent access. CAISI states that responses from AI agent deployers, developers, and computer security researchers will inform future voluntary guidelines and best practices, and will contribute to CAISI's ongoing research and evaluations of agent security. Respondents are encouraged to submit concrete examples, case studies, and actionable recommendations based on direct experience with AI agent systems. Comments are to be submitted via www.regulations.gov under docket no. NIST-2025-0035.
rows.body_name: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation
